EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of very deep low pressure moved northeast along the south shore of Lake Erie during the late evening hours of November 16th and early morning hours of November 17th.  Strong and gusty winds accompanied this storm system.  Some damage was reported to the east of Cleveland in Lake County.   A peak 61 mph gust was reported in Lake County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts as high 60 mph toppled a large sign and a utility pole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread light to moderate snow fell during the daylight hours of January 7th as an Alberta Clipper tracked through the region.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged between 3 and 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow developed around daybreak January 7th and became moderate to heavy during the late morning and early afternoon hours as a clipper system moved through the region.  The snow tapered off to just a few lingering snow showers and flurries by late in the afternoon.  Total snow accumulations of 3.0 to 4.5 inches were common across the county.  The accumulating snow and low visibilities led to a few accidents and slide-offs across the area, along with cancellations of local events.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast across northern Indiana during the evening hours of April 7th.  A warm front stretching east from the low swept north across northern Ohio.  Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along this front.  A couple of the stronger thunderstorms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms winds downed several trees in McComb.  A propane tank was blown over at a residence.  Garage doors on a couple homes in the town were also blown in.  Peak wind gusts were estimated to be almost 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast across northern Indiana during the evening hours of April 7th.  A warm front stretching east from the low swept north across northern Ohio. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed one tree and several large tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow and strong winds resulted in low visibilities and drifting snow February 5th into early February 6th, as a rapidly intensifying area of low pressure tracked eastward through the lower Ohio Valley.  Snowfall totals generally ranged between 4 and 8 inches, with drifts as high as 3 to 4 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow developed during the early afternoon hours on February 5th, and continued into the early morning hours of February 6th.  Gusty winds accompanied the snow, with gusts of 30 to 45 mph limiting visibilities.  The falling snow and strong winds also led to plenty of blowing and drifting snow, with drifts of 2 to 4 feet reported.  Snowfall totals across the county generally ranged between 6 and 8 inches.  There were reports of slide-offs and accidents across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure in the Mississippi Valley strengthened as it moved into the Ohio Valley early on Tuesday, February 9th. Snow began on Monday night and spread northeast early on Tuesday. Later in the afternoon, this snow mixed with rain south of the Ohio River. As the system pulled out of the region Tuesday night, colder air on strong winds changed the precipitation back to snow, with additional accumulations noted in blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell in Chillicothe. Law enforcement in Alma measured 4.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Only 3 days after an historic snow storm hit the region, another major storm blasted portions of western Pennyslvania, northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and eastern Ohio from the afternoon of February 9th to the early morning of February 11th. Blizzard conditions occurred in Garrett county Maryland, Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia, and in the higher elevations of Fayette and Westmoreland counties in Pennsylvania. Over 2 feet of snow fell across Garrett, Preston and Tucker counties with 9 to 24 inches of snow in Fayette and Westmoreland counties. Snowfall amounts ranged from 28 inches at Red House, MD to 26 inches at Davis, WV, to 19 inches in Farmington, PA. Winds gusting over 35 MPH in the higher elevations caused whiteout conditions at times with visibilities below one quarter mile for an extended period. Elsewhere in western Pennsylvania snowfall was from 8 to 12 inches generally in and north of Pittsburgh to north of I-80. An isolated area of heavier snow also fell in and around New Philadelphia in Tuscarawas county OH, with 8 inches of snow in New Cumberland, OH. In the remainder of eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia 3 to 5 inches of snow fell.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four and a half inches of snow fell in Brookville, while Centerville saw 4 inches. A spotter southwest of Centerville measured 3.4 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low presure moved east across the Ohio Valley the afternoon of the 15th spreading snow across much of eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Snow continued in northwest flow behind the storm into the 18th with storm totals in the ridges of northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland from 12 to 18 inches. Some snow amounts across eastern Ohio were from 6 to 9 inches, with 6 to 10 inches in the ridges of Westmoreland and Fayette counties in Pennsylvania. A general storm total of 4 to 7 inches was reported across the remainder of western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.

Snow continued in northwest flow behind the storm into the 18th with storm totals in the ridges of northern West Virginia and Garrett county Maryland from 12 to 18 inches. Some snow amounts across eastern Ohio were from 6 to 9 inches, with 6 to 10 inches in the ridges of Westmoreland and Fayette counties in Pennsylvania. A general storm total of 4 to 7 inches was reported across the remainder of western Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia.

Low pressure in the Mississippi Valley strengthened as it moved into the Ohio Valley early on Tuesday, February 9th. Snow began on Monday night and spread northeast early on Tuesday. Later in the afternoon, this snow mixed with rain south of the Ohio River. As the system pulled out of the region Tuesday night, colder air on strong winds changed the precipitation back to snow, with additional accumulations noted in blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports from Centerville were 6.5 and 7.5 inches, with 5.4 inches recorded at the Dayton international airport KDAY.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed across the region during the morning hours of Sunday, December 12th as a strong low pressure system moved through the Great Lakes. The snow continued into Sunday night before tapering off during the morning hours of Monday, December 13th. Much of the area saw 2 to 4 inches. Heavier amounts of up to 6 inches fell between Dayton and Cincinnati. Additionally, westerly winds of 10-20 mph with higher gusts caused blowing and drifting of the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters located two and five miles northeast of Hamilton measured 5.7 and 5.4 inches of snow, respectively. South of Hamilton 5 inches was measured, while 4 inches fell west of Mason and 3 inches fell in Oxford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure in the Mississippi Valley strengthened as it moved into the Ohio Valley early on Tuesday, February 9th. Snow began on Monday night and spread northeast early on Tuesday. Later in the afternoon, this snow mixed with rain south of the Ohio River. As the system pulled out of the region Tuesday night, colder air on strong winds changed the precipitation back to snow, with additional accumulations noted in blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved east across central Ohio during the evening hours of May 11th.  Showers and thunderstorms developed along and north of a warm front stretching east from the low.  A few of the thunderstorms became severe and at least two tornadoes occurred.  The tornadoes caused minor damage in Sandusky and Morrow Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down in rural Sandusky County just southeast of Fremont.  The initial touchdown was just north of the intersection of Buchanan and McGormley Roads.  The tornado then traveled northeast across open land for just over four and a half miles before lifting near the intersection of Beeler and Flora Roads.  Many trees were downed along the damage path which was intermittent and no more than 50 yards in width.  A few barns and outbuildings along the damage path sustained minor damage.

A cold front moved slowly through the area during the early evening hours. While overall instability was modest, strong wind fields were in place. An area of storms developed in far eastern Indiana and tracked northeast into Paulding county. This area produced sporadic damage across the county before weakening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported west facing barn doors were blown into the barn with north facing doors blown out.  Damage was estimated at $10,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved slowly through the area during the early evening hours. While overall instability was modest, strong wind fields were in place. An area of storms developed in far eastern Indiana and tracked northeast into Paulding county. This area produced sporadic damage across the county before weakening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported that damage was observed along a roughly half mile long path. The damage started near the intersection of county highway C-105 and U.S. 24. Two mobile homes that were attached together, were flipped over, destroying it. As the wind continued east a pole barn next to the mobile home was destroyed. Along U.S. 24, several trees were uprooted in two separate residences. Two homes suffered roof damage as well. The damage ended just across the Maumee River where several large broken tree limbs were found along the river bank. The combined damage is estimated at $100,000.

Motion of the storms was fairly quick and towards the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

After a cold and rainy start to the day, low pressure tracked towards the area, reaching Defiance, Ohio early Friday evening. Strong shear and moderate instability was in place across the area, allowing for two supercells to develop across far eastern Indiana and track northeast into Ohio. Wind damage was reported in several counties in northwestern Ohio.

A weak frontal boundary pushed south into the region with modest instability and shear. The storms were generally poorly organized but managed to produce some pockets of strong to severe wind.  One brief tornado touchdown also occurred with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were broken due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a cold and rainy start to the day, low pressure tracked towards the area, reaching Defiance, Ohio early Friday evening. Strong shear and moderate instability was in place across the area, allowing for two supercells to develop across far eastern Indiana and track northeast into Ohio. Wind damage was reported in several counties in northwestern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported tennis ball size hail, as well as estimated wind gusts up to 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure was moving from Illinois into Indiana during the afternoon and evening hours. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level impulse on the northern periphery of a ridge resulted in scattered thunderstorms during the evening. A large complex of thunderstorms over Indiana weakened as it pushed into Ohio, but still managed to produce a strong outflow boundary which raced out ahead of the storms.  The strongest winds occurred where the outflow rushed out fastest, despite a lack of high velocity and sometimes a lack of storms on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved southeast across the upper Ohio Valley, scattered severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia. A supercell thunderstorm produced an EF1 tornado in Carroll and Jefferson counties in Ohio, with numerous reports of wind damage and hail up to golf ball size elsewhere. Flooding of streams and creeks also occurred over eastern Ohio and the northern panhandle of West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A county official reported numerous roads closed due to flooding in Wellsville.

A slow moving cold front interacted with moderate instability, abundant moisture and a weak flow to allow for thunderstorm development. While the majority of the storms were not overly strong, the slow movement and efficient precipitation production allowed for training of the activity and flash flooding in some areas of northwestern Ohio. Radar estimates of 4 to 6 inches were noted.

A weak frontal boundary pushed south into the region with modest instability and shear. The storms were generally poorly organized but managed to produce some pockets of strong to severe wind.  One brief tornado touchdown also occurred with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Columbus metro traffic camera at the intersection of Interstate 270 and Broad Street recorded a very brief tornado touchdown just north of this location.  No reports of damage were received in the immediate area, but the video footage does show a brief tornadic circulation in contact with the ground.  Winds with this tornado are estimated to be around 65 miles per hour.

Rainfall ended across the area during the late evening hours of the 27th, but the road closures continued for several more hours before subsiding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level system and moderate instability allowed for thunderstorms to develop. A lack of overall shear resulted in the storms producing isolated wind damage as cells collapsed or cold pools established.

An area of strong low pressure moved up the Ohio Valley on December 12th.  This low shifted to New England by early on the 13th.  Cold north to northwest winds behind this low caused lake effect snow showers to develop over Northeast Ohio during the afternoon hours of December 12th.  The snow activity intensified on December 13th with periods of moderate to heavy snow during the morning and afternoon hours.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 30 mph on the 13th.  The strong winds and snow combined to create near zero visibilities and considerable blowing and drifting.  Snowfall rates with the heaviest bands approached two inches per hour.  The heaviest snowfall on the 13th fell on southeastern Cuyahoga County, southern Geauga County and the northern portions of Summit and Portage Counties.  The lake effect activity lessened during the late evening hours of the 13th and early morning hours of the 14th.  The activity picked up again during the morning of the 14th over inland Ashtabula County and northeastern Trumbull County.  The snow finally tapered off from west to east late on the 14th and early on the 15th.  Snowfall totals over much of the Northeast Ohio snowbelt were in excess of a foot from the 12th through the 15th.  Peak totals of over two feet were reported in Portage and Trumbull Counties.  Peak totals for each of the counties affected were:  29.5 inches at Kinsman in Trumbull County;  25.3 inches at Kent in Portage County; 22.3 inches at Roaming Shores in inland Ashtabula County; 21.2 inches at Twinsburg in Summit County; 19.0 inched in Russell Township in Geauga County; 16.8 inches at Solon in Cuyahoga County; 12.0 inches at South Madison in Lake County and 9.5 inches at Medina in Medina County.  Officially, 14.5 inches of snow was measured at the Youngstown-Warren Regional Airport in Trumbull County on the 12th through the 14th.  Drifts of up to three feet deep were reported in some areas.  Most of the snow in Lake and Medina Counties fell on the 13th.  Travel during this event was treacherous at times.  Many schools were closed or delayed on the 13th and 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snow fell on portions of Trumbull County from the afternoon hours of December 12th through the early morning hours of December 15th.  Snowfall totals ranged from just a few inches in the southeastern corner of the county to over two feet in the northeast.  A peak total of 29.5 inches was reported at Kinsman.  Snowfall rates with the heaviest bands of snow approached two inches per hour.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 30 mph on the 13th.  The snow and gusty winds combined to create near zero visibilities and considerable blowing and drifting.  Travel was treacherous during this event.  Officially, 14.5 inches of snow was measured at the Youngstown-Warren Regional Airport on December 12th through the 14th.  Many school districts were closed or delayed on the 13th and 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved east across Northeast Ohio late on December 31st.  Cold north to northwest winds behind the front caused lake effect snow showers to develop by midnight on the 1st.  Intermittent snow showers were then reported for several days.  This activity finally diminished during the afternoon hours of January 5th.   Moderate to heavy snow was reported at times with snowfall rates approaching two inches per hour with the most intense bands.  On January 1st and 2nd, the heaviest snow fell over northern Geauga and inland Ashtabula Counties.  An upper level disturbance moved across the region late on the 2nd and early on the 3rd.  This caused winds to become more northerly resulting in snow showers spreading west across Lake and eastern Cuyahoga Counties.  Heavy snow was reported across Cleveland's eastern suburbs on the 3rd.  By late on the 3rd and early on the 4th, heavy lake snow showers began to affect the northern portions of Summit, Portage and Trumbull Counties.  Drier air finally began to move into the area on January 5th causing the snow showers to dissipate late in the day.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 25 mph on the 2nd and 3rd.  These winds combined with the snow caused near whiteout conditions at times.  Snowfall amounts varied considerably across Northeast Ohio during this event.  Totals ranged from just a few inches over western Cuyahoga County to over five feet across inland Ashtabula County.  Travel was severely hampered during this event and hundreds of accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers began in Geauga County just after daybreak on January 1st.  Moderate to heavy snow was reported at times from midday through the late evening hours of the 1st.  Snow showers continued on the 2nd but most of the snow was light.  At least six inches of snow was reported over much of northern Geauga County by late evening on the 1st.  Another couple inches fell from the early morning hours of the 2nd through the evening of the 2nd.  Snowfall totals on the 1st and 2nd ranged from just a couple inches in the southern part of the county to 6 to 10 inches in the north.  A peak total of 10.5 inches was measured near Montville.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 25 mph on the 2nd which caused considerable blowing and drifting.  Travel was hampered during this event and a few accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent cold northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie resulted in a prolonged period of moderate to heavy lake effect snow in Northeast Ohio.  Lake effect snow showers developed during the late evening hours of December 4th.  This activity quickly intensified on the 5th and then continued into December 7th before finally diminishing late in the day.  Intense bands of snow were reported on both the 5th and 6th.  Snowfall rates at times were in excess of two inches per hour with visibilities near zero.  Snowfall totals across northeastern Ohio varied considerably.  More than two feet of snow was reported over much of Geauga County, portions of northern Summit and Portage Counties, northwestern Trumbull County and southeastern Cuyahoga County.   Locations further west and south generally saw no more than a few inches of snow from the 5th through the 7th.  Scattered snow showers persisted into December 8th, but little additional accumulation was reported through midday.  A band of intense lake effect snow developed early in the afternoon over Cuyahoga County.  This band stretched from downtown Cleveland eastward into Geauga County.   Six to as much as ten inches of snow fell on the northern half of the Cleveland metropolitan area during the evening commute.  Traffic in the city was reduced to a crawl.  Many motorists ran out of the gas on area streets and freeways and were forced to abandon their vehicles.  People reported needing an hour to travel just a few blocks.  Some commuters needed as much as four hours to get home from work.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 35 mph during the first half of this event which caused blizzard like conditions at times.  Drifts over three feet deep were reported during this event and travel was nearly impossible at times.   Peak snowfall totals for the counties affected by this event included:  33.0 inches at Burton in Geauga County; 28.0 inches at Hiram in northeast Portage County; 27.3 inches at Solon in southeastern Cuyahoga County; 27.0 inches at Mespotamia in northwestern Trumbull County; 24.3 inches at Sagamore Hills in northern Summit County and 18.8 inches at Brunswick in northern Medina County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snow fell on northern half of Portage County from late in the evening on December 4th through the evening hours of the 7th.  Snowfall totals ranged from 15 to 20 inches over the northern half of the county.  A peak total of 28.0 inches was measured at Hiram in the northeastern corner of the county.  Snowfall rates with the heaviest bands of snow exceeded two inches per hour.  Strong northwest winds accompanied the snow and caused blizzard like conditions at times.  Travel was severely hampered during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed across the region during the morning hours of Sunday, December 12th as a strong low pressure system moved through the Great Lakes. The snow continued into Sunday night before tapering off during the morning hours of Monday, December 13th. Much of the area saw 2 to 4 inches. Heavier amounts of up to 6 inches fell between Dayton and Cincinnati. Additionally, westerly winds of 10-20 mph with higher gusts caused blowing and drifting of the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An employee east of Lebanon measured 5 inches of snow. Spotters in Mason and Kings Mills measured 3.7 and 3.3 inches, respectively.

More than two feet of snow was reported over much of Geauga County, portions of northern Summit and Portage Counties, northwestern Trumbull County and southeastern Cuyahoga County.   Locations further west and south generally saw no more than a few inches of snow from the 5th through the 7th.  Scattered snow showers persisted into December 8th, but little additional accumulation was reported through midday.  A band of intense lake effect snow developed early in the afternoon over Cuyahoga County.  This band stretched from downtown Cleveland eastward into Geauga County.   Six to as much as ten inches of snow fell on the northern half of the Cleveland metropolitan area during the evening commute.  Traffic in the city was reduced to a crawl.  Many motorists ran out of the gas on area streets and freeways and were forced to abandon their vehicles.  People reported needing an hour to travel just a few blocks.  Some commuters needed as much as four hours to get home from work.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 35 mph during the first half of this event which caused blizzard like conditions at times.  Drifts over three feet deep were reported during this event and travel was nearly impossible at times.   Peak snowfall totals for the counties affected by this event included:  33.0 inches at Burton in Geauga County; 28.0 inches at Hiram in northeast Portage County; 27.3 inches at Solon in southeastern Cuyahoga County; 27.0 inches at Mespotamia in northwestern Trumbull County; 24.3 inches at Sagamore Hills in northern Summit County and 18.8 inches at Brunswick in northern Medina County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snow fell on portions of Ashtabula County from late in the evening on December 4th through the evening hours of the 7th.  Snowfall totals ranged from 12 to 20 inches over most of inland Ashtabula County.  A peak total of 23.7 inches was reported near Pierpont with 22.1 inches at Windsor.  Snowfall rates with the heaviest bands of snow exceeded two inches per hour.  Strong northwest winds accompanied the snow and caused blizzard like conditions at times.  Travel was severely hampered during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved east across Northeast Ohio late on December 31st.  Cold north to northwest winds behind the front caused lake effect snow showers to develop by midnight on the 1st.  Intermittent snow showers were then reported for several days.  This activity finally diminished during the afternoon hours of January 5th.   Moderate to heavy snow was reported at times with snowfall rates approaching two inches per hour with the most intense bands.  On January 1st and 2nd, the heaviest snow fell over northern Geauga and inland Ashtabula Counties.  An upper level disturbance moved across the region late on the 2nd and early on the 3rd.  This caused winds to become more northerly resulting in snow showers spreading west across Lake and eastern Cuyahoga Counties.  Heavy snow was reported across Cleveland's eastern suburbs on the 3rd.  By late on the 3rd and early on the 4th, heavy lake snow showers began to affect the northern portions of Summit, Portage and Trumbull Counties.  Drier air finally began to move into the area on January 5th causing the snow showers to dissipate late in the day.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 25 mph on the 2nd and 3rd.  These winds combined with the snow caused near whiteout conditions at times.  Snowfall amounts varied considerably across Northeast Ohio during this event.  Totals ranged from just a few inches over western Cuyahoga County to over five feet across inland Ashtabula County.  Travel was severely hampered during this event and hundreds of accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Occasional lake effect snow showers affected Trumbull County on January 1st and 2nd.  Most of this snow was light with only small accumulations reported.  The snow showers began to increase in intensity and coverage during the morning hours of January 3rd.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow were reported from midday on the  3rd through the morning hours of the 4th.  From the evening of the 4th through the morning of the 5th the snow shower activity was very scattered with most areas seeing only occasional light snow or flurries.  During this event, the heaviest snow fell on the northern portion on the county.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 25 mph on the 3rd.  These strong winds resulted in considerable blowing and drifting along with visibilities near zero at times.  Snowfall totals across Trumbull County on the 3rd and 4th ranged from just a couple of inches at the southern end of the county to over a foot in the north.  A peak two day total of 14.0 inches was reported at Mesopotamia in the northwest corner of the county.  A spotter in Kinsman measured 10.5 inches of snow on the 3rd and 4th.  Travel was hampered during this event and many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from the southern plains eastward through the Tennessee Valley on Thursday, December 16th. A significant amount of moist air was pulled into the Ohio Valley ahead of this surface low, diminishing significantly as it went further north past the Ohio River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow was reported in both West Union and in Blue Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong low pressure moved up the Ohio Valley on December 12th.  This low shifted to New England by early on the 13th.  Cold north to northwest winds behind this low caused lake effect snow showers to develop over Northeast Ohio during the afternoon hours of December 12th.  The snow activity intensified on December 13th with periods of moderate to heavy snow during the morning and afternoon hours.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 30 mph on the 13th.  The strong winds and snow combined to create near zero visibilities and considerable blowing and drifting.  Snowfall rates with the heaviest bands approached two inches per hour.  The heaviest snowfall on the 13th fell on southeastern Cuyahoga County, southern Geauga County and the northern portions of Summit and Portage Counties.  The lake effect activity lessened during the late evening hours of the 13th and early morning hours of the 14th.  The activity picked up again during the morning of the 14th over inland Ashtabula County and northeastern Trumbull County.  The snow finally tapered off from west to east late on the 14th and early on the 15th.  Snowfall totals over much of the Northeast Ohio snowbelt were in excess of a foot from the 12th through the 15th.  Peak totals of over two feet were reported in Portage and Trumbull Counties.  Peak totals for each of the counties affected were:  29.5 inches at Kinsman in Trumbull County;  25.3 inches at Kent in Portage County; 22.3 inches at Roaming Shores in inland Ashtabula County; 21.2 inches at Twinsburg in Summit County; 19.0 inched in Russell Township in Geauga County; 16.8 inches at Solon in Cuyahoga County; 12.0 inches at South Madison in Lake County and 9.5 inches at Medina in Medina County.  Officially, 14.5 inches of snow was measured at the Youngstown-Warren Regional Airport in Trumbull County on the 12th through the 14th.  Drifts of up to three feet deep were reported in some areas.  Most of the snow in Lake and Medina Counties fell on the 13th.  Travel during this event was treacherous at times.  Many schools were closed or delayed on the 13th and 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect snow fell on the northern half of Medina County from the afternoon hours of December 12th through midday on the 14th.  The heaviest snow fell on the 13th.  A peak total of 9.5 inches was reported at Medina.  Snowfall rates on the 13th were as much as an inch per hour.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 30 mph on the 13th.  The snow and gusty winds combined to create near zero visibilities and considerable blowing and drifting.  Travel was treacherous during this event.  Many school districts were closed or delayed on the 13th and 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed across the region during the morning hours of Sunday, December 12th as a strong low pressure system moved through the Great Lakes. The snow continued into Sunday night before tapering off during the morning hours of Monday, December 13th. Much of the area saw 2 to 4 inches. Heavier amounts of up to 6 inches fell between Dayton and Cincinnati. Additionally, westerly winds of 10-20 mph with higher gusts caused blowing and drifting of the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter located 6 miles east-northeast of West Union measured 2.3 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of a line of thunderstorms that moved through much of Lower Michigan spread into northwestern Ohio, allowing for a few thunderstorms to affect the area.  Some of these did become locally severe, but dissipated by mid afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple tree branches were down across SR 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front worked north towards Lower Michigan during the evening hours of the 5th. Storms were initially slow to organize, but quickly intensified and became super-cellular. One of these storms tracked into Fulton County and quickly spawned a tornado north of Liberty Center. The tornado remained on the ground for roughly 7 miles producing damage along its track before dissipating near the Fulton/Lucas county line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down along Highway 109 just north of County Road A and moved northeast, impacting several homes, trees and power lines. Several homes were severely damaged or destroyed (DI:FR12 DOD: 8). However, improper anchoring of some of the homes resulted in a failure on all walls, resulting in their destruction. Several trees were uprooted or damaged, including through the Maumee State Forest. The tornado ended near the Fulton/Lucas county line. The maximum width of the tornado was around 700 yards with maximum wind speeds estimated around 130 mph. Damage is estimated at $500,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe storms advanced from Illinois and intensified further as it interacted with strong instability and shear. The line took on a line echo wave pattern (LEWP), resulting in an abundance of wind damage reports, including a focused micro-burst in Williams County. A brief tornado also developed in Van Wert County, resulting in some damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines and small tree branches downed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intense microburst with estimated winds at 100 mph was surveyed in the town of Edgerton.  A large portion of the upper story of the old town hall building next to the fire department was blown in with loss of much of the roof.  Debris from this structure damaged nearby vehicles and the flea market building across the street including a portion of the south-facing wall getting blown out.  One person was injured at this location from falling debris. In addition, a freight container was blown off an intermodal train and there was significant tree damage in town. Wind damage path was about 3 miles long and a quarter of a mile wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta clipper tracked southeast through the Ohio Valley on Thursday, January 7th. Very cold air was in place, which allowed for a very fluffy snow to accumulate over a prolonged period of time. Snow totals from this event ranged from two to five inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county garage measured 4 inches of snow in Chillicothe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continuous rain, mixed with sleet at times, fell during the daylight hours of Friday the 5th.  The exception was further north across Morgan and Perry Counties, where  mostly wet snow occurred.  The rain changed to snow from north to south during that evening.  Six to 12 inches of snow fell across Morgan and Perry Counties with the higher range mostly across northern portions of Perry County.  For example, Glenford and Thornville reported 12 inches or more.  Meanwhile, the snow accumulation at New Lexington was 8 inches, and 6 inches at McConnelsville.  Further south, snow accumulations of 4 to 7 inches were more common across Jackson, Vinton, Athens, Meigs, and Washington Counties.

Low pressure tracked from the Gulf of Mexico to the southern Appalachians on Friday, February 5th. A wintery mix overspread much of the region early on Friday and transitioned to all snow, bringing significant snowfall to the area on Saturday, February 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county garage in Wapakoneta measured 9.5 inches of snowfall on the morning of the 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from the Gulf of Mexico to the southern Appalachians on Friday, February 5th. A wintery mix overspread much of the region early on Friday and transitioned to all snow, bringing significant snowfall to the area on Saturday, February 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county garage in Urbana measured a foot of snow. A spotter in Saint Paris measured 10.3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A center of low pressure tracked south of the Ohio Valley on Monday, February 15th. As it approached the area, snow overspread the region, accumulating from 4 to 8 inches, with the heaviest amounts found along the I-71 corridor between Cincinnati and Columbus.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of snow was measured in Wapakoneta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved east across Northeast Ohio late on December 31st.  Cold north to northwest winds behind the front caused lake effect snow showers to develop by midnight on the 1st.  Intermittent snow showers were then reported for several days.  This activity finally diminished during the afternoon hours of January 5th.   Moderate to heavy snow was reported at times with snowfall rates approaching two inches per hour with the most intense bands.  On January 1st and 2nd, the heaviest snow fell over northern Geauga and inland Ashtabula Counties.  An upper level disturbance moved across the region late on the 2nd and early on the 3rd.  This caused winds to become more northerly resulting in snow showers spreading west across Lake and eastern Cuyahoga Counties.  Heavy snow was reported across Cleveland's eastern suburbs on the 3rd.  By late on the 3rd and early on the 4th, heavy lake snow showers began to affect the northern portions of Summit, Portage and Trumbull Counties.  Drier air finally began to move into the area on January 5th causing the snow showers to dissipate late in the day.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 25 mph on the 2nd and 3rd.  These winds combined with the snow caused near whiteout conditions at times.  Snowfall amounts varied considerably across Northeast Ohio during this event.  Totals ranged from just a few inches over western Cuyahoga County to over five feet across inland Ashtabula County.  Travel was severely hampered during this event and hundreds of accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers began in Ashtabula County during the morning of January 1st.  Moderate to heavy snow showers then continued through the afternoon hours of the 4th.  From the evening of the 4th through midday on the 5th the snow shower activity was very scattered with most areas seeing only occasional light snow or flurries.  Some of the heaviest snow fell during the afternoon of the 3rd with snowfall rates approaching two inches per hour at times.  Northwest winds gusted to as much as 25 mph on the 2nd and 3rd.  These strong winds resulted in considerable blowing and drifting along with visibilities near zero at times.  Snowfall totals across inland Ashtabula County varied considerably during this five day event.  Locations near the southern border of the county generally saw between 12 and 15 inches of snow with remainder of the county generally seeing between 15 and 30 inches of snow.  Peak totals in the county were found over the east-central portion of the county.  A spotter near Pierpont measured an event total of 61.3 inches.  Nearly 30 inches of that total fell from the early morning hours of the third through daybreak on the 4th.  Drifts as much as 8 feet tall were reported in the county.  Travel was severely hampered during this event and many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deeper moisture wrapped south, around a coastal storm, late on Friday the 26th into Saturday the 27th.  A snow accumulation of 4 to 7 inches occurred.

An Alberta clipper tracked southeast through the Ohio Valley on Thursday, January 7th. Very cold air was in place, which allowed for a very fluffy snow to accumulate over a prolonged period of time. Snow totals from this event ranged from two to five inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow was measured in Seaman, while 3.7 inches was measured in West Union by evening.

Heavier snow finally arrived on the 3rd with periods of moderate to heavy reported through midday on the 4th. Most locations north of Interstate 90 saw between 6 and 10 inches of snow during that period.  A peak total for the event of 21.1 inches was measured near Conneaut. Travel was severely hampered during this event and many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe storms advanced from Illinois and intensified further as it interacted with strong instability and shear. The line took on a line echo wave pattern (LEWP), resulting in an abundance of wind damage reports, including a focused micro-burst in Williams County. A brief tornado also developed in Van Wert County, resulting in some damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials surveyed an area of damage south of Van Wert and determined a circulation touched down in a wheat field a quarter mile west of Dustman Drive and US 127. The circulation continued along Dustman Road, with most of the damage being to trees. A few limbs fell onto homes, causing minor damage. In addition, some shingle damage occurred. The roof of a barn was torn off near US 127 as well. Winds are estimated to be between 80 and 85 mph with a maximum width of the tornado of around 50 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening area of low pressure tracked eastward near the Interstate 80 corridor in Indiana and Ohio. Precipitation initially began as rain on December 11th, before transitioning to heavy snow on the 12th as colder air filtered in on the backside of this low pressure system. Snowfall totals generally ranged between 3 and 6 inches. Wind gusts of 25 to 35 mph created widespread blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to snow during the morning hours of December 12th and continued through the entire day. The snow was heavy, at times reducing visibilities to near a quarter of a mile.  Snowfall totals were generally between 4 and 5 inches, with the emergency manager reporting 4.3 inches of total snow accumulation in Van Wert.  Winds increased Sunday afternoon into Monday morning, with gusts of 25 to 35 mph at times. This, combined with the fallen snow, created widespread blowing and drifting snow that led to numerous car accidents and school closings across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong low pressure moved north from the Gulf Coast to Kentucky on February 5th.  The low lingered over eastern Kentucky from much of the 6th before shifting to the Mid Atlantic Coast late in the day.  Snow associated with this low spread north across northern Ohio during the afternoon of the 5th.  The snow began in Knox and Morrow Counties just before midday and reached the U.S. Route 30 corridor around 2 pm.  By late afternoon the snow had reached the southern suburbs of Cleveland.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow were reported from the later part of the afternoon through around daybreak on the 6th.  The snow quickly ended from northwest to southeast during the morning of the 6th.  Northeast winds of 15 to 25 mph accompanied the snow.  Winds gusted to more than 30 mph at times during the late evening hours of the 5th and early morning hours of the 6th.  Near blizzard conditions with visibilities less than a quarter mile were reported at times.  Snowfall rates exceeded an inch per hour for much of this event.  The heaviest snow fell from the Mansfield area northeast into the Youngstown and Warren areas.  More than a foot of snow fell in that area with a few locations reporting as much as 16 inches of snow.  Accumulations dropped off slightly further north and south.  Considerable blowing and drifting was also reported.  Travel during the peak of this event was nearly impossible and many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow spread into Ashland County from the south during the early afternoon hours of February 5th and then increased in intensity during the middle part of the afternoon.  Moderate to heavy snow then continued through daybreak on the 6th with the snow eventually ending during the morning hours.  Snowfall rates during much of this event exceeded an inch per hour.  Northeast winds gusted to as much as 30 mph from the evening of the 5th through the early morning hours of the 6th.  The snow and strong winds combined to create near blizzard conditions with visibilities less than one quarter mile at times.  Snowfall totals during this event ranged from 12 to 16 inches across most of Ashland County.  A spotter in Sullivan reported a storm total of 13 inches with drifts three to five feet deep.  Travel was nearly impossible at the peak of this event.  Many accidents were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow spread into Geauga County from the south during the middle afternoon hours of February 5th and then increased in intensity during the evening hours.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow were then reported during the evening hours of the 5th and early morning hours of the 6th.  The snow eventually tapered off just after daybreak on the 6th.  Snowfall rates at times exceeded an inch per hour.  Northeast winds gusted to as much as 30 mph from the evening of the 5th through the early morning hours of the 6th.  The snow and strong winds combined to create near blizzard conditions with visibilities less than one quarter mile at times.  Snowfall totals in Geauga County ranged from just a few inches in the northwestern corner of the county to around 10 inches along the southern border.  Storm total reports received from snow spotters included: 10.4 inches near Bainbridge; 7.0 inches at Burton; 6.3 inches in Montville Township and 6.0 inches in Russell Township.  Snow drifts over two feet deep were reported in southern Geauga County and travel was very difficult during the peak of this storm.  Many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong low pressure developed off the Mid Atlantic Coast early on February 25th.  This low deepened considerably and moved slowly up the East Coast on the 25th and 26th.  A trough stretching northwest from this low lingered over the Upper Ohio Valley from February 25th into the 27th.  Cold north to northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie in association with the trough caused lake effect snow showers to develop and intensify on the 25th.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow then affected portions of Northeast Ohio through the afternoon hours of the 26th.  The snow finally diminished during the evening hours of 26th.  Snowfall totals over portions of Northeast Ohio exceeded a foot on the 25th and 26th.  Northwest winds gusted to more than 30 mph on both the 25th and 26th.  Peak gusts of around 35 mph occurred during the early morning hours of the 26th.  The wind and snow combined to create near blizzard conditions at times with visibilities less than one quarter mile.   Drifts up to three feet deep were reported at a few locations.  Some light freezing rain and drizzle also mixed with the snow at times during the afternoon of the 26th.  A slight coating of ice was reported at a few locations.  This storm made travel very difficult and many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow showers developed over Lake County during the morning hours of February 25th and quickly intensified.  A period of moderate to heavy snow occurred from late morning into the early afternoon hours.  The snow showers then diminished with mainly light snow reported during the second half of the afternoon and most of the evening.  The snow showers began to increase in intensity again just after midnight on the 26th with periods of moderate to heavy snow then occurring into the afternoon.  Snowfall rates at times exceeded an inch per hour.  Areas of light freezing rain and drizzle mixed with the snow showers during the afternoon of the 26th.  A slight glazing of ice was reported at a few locations.  More light snow fell late in the afternoon and during the evening of the 26th.  The snow showers finally tapered to flurries late in the evening.  In addition, northwest winds gusted to more than 30 mph on both the 25th and 26th.  Peak gusts of around 35 mph occurred during the early morning hours of the 26th.  The wind and snow combined to create near blizzard conditions at times with visibilities less than one quarter mile.  Snow totals across Lake County during this event ranged from around 6 inches at the western end of the county to more than 15 inches in the east.  Snowfall totals from the 25th and 26th received from snowfall spotters included: 17.7 inches in Concord Township; 14.5 inches in Perry Township and 13.5 inches at Mentor.  Drifts up to three feet deep were reported at a few locations.  This storm made travel very difficult and many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercells developed along a warm front initially affecting northern Kentucky during the late afternoon hours. Activity increased and developed into multicell clusters as it lifted northeast across the area.

An area of low pressure centered over Tennessee at daybreak on February 9th moved rapidly northeast during the day.  By early evening, the low was centered over Central Ohio.  The low continued to move northeast during the evening hours and exited the state just after midnight on February 10th.  Snow associated with this low spread into northern Ohio a couple hours before sunrise on the 9th.  The snow continued most of the 9th before briefly tapering to flurries or freezing drizzle during the evening hours.  Light to moderate snow resumed before midnight on the 10th and then continued into the morning hours.  The snow tapered to flurries in the Toledo area late in the morning, but didn't quit in the Mansfield area till late afternoon.  The heaviest snow fell in Northwest Ohio where accumulations in Lucas County topped a foot.  Storm total accumulations dropped off considerably to the south and east of Toledo.  Heavy snow was also reported in Ashtabula County where Lake Erie helped to enhance the snowfall.  Snow began in Ashtabula County during the afternoon of the 9th and then increased in intensity during the early morning hours of the 10th when winds became westerly.  Heavy snow was reported during the morning hours of the 10th with the snow finally tapering off during the evening.  Local accumulations of more than 10 inches were reported in Ashtabula County.   Winds across northern Ohio went from easterly to southwest or west during the evening hours of the 9th as the low moved through.  Gusts to 25 mph began soon after this transition occurred.  Winds remained strong through the early morning hours of the 10th.  Considerable blowing and drifting was reported in Northwest Ohio making difficult driving conditions even worse.  Many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in Wood County a couple hours before sunrise on February 9th.  The snow picked up in intensity late in the morning with periods of moderate to heavy snow then reported into early evening.  The intensity of the snow lessened during the evening hours with light snow finally ending late in the morning of February 10th.  Snowfall rates at the peak of the storm were around an inch per hour with visibilities less than one half mile.   Total snow accumulations across Wood County ranged from 6 to 10 inches with the highest amounts in the northwest corner of the county.  A cooperative observer in Bowling Green measured a storm total of 8.0 inches.  A snow spotter in Perrysburg measured 6.2 inches of snow for the event.  East to southeast winds accompanied the snow as it fell.  Winds eventually became southwest and increased during the evening hours of the 9th.  Winds gusted to as much as 25 mph from the late evening hours of the 9th through the early morning hours of the 10th.  The winds and snow combined to create considerable blowing and drifting.  Travel was severely hampered by this storm and many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure moving north from the Gulf of Mexico on February 5th combined with low pressure across the Ohio Valley and intensified over the Mid-Atlantic states. This produced an historic snow storm across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and eastern Ohio from the afternoon of February 5th to the afternoon of February 6th. Over 2 feet of snow fell across portions of Garrett county Maryland, and in Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia, with locally 3 feet in some locations. Storm totals ranged from 37 inches in Friendsville, MD to 22 inches in Terra Alta, WV. Across Pennsylvania, 18 to 24 inches of snow fell roughly between an area from SR 422 and I-70, with 21.1 inches of snow falling in Pittsburgh. This was the 4th largest snowfall on record in Pittsburgh. Over 2 feet of snow fell in the ridges of Westmoreland and Fayette counties in Pennsylvania. Along and north of I-80 in Pennsylvania 6 to 18 inches of snow fell. South of I-70 in Pennsylvania, eastern Ohio and northern West Virginia amounts ranged from 10 to 20 inches. North of I-70, in eastern Ohio, snow accumulated 12 to 18 inches. The snow was wet and heavy in Pittsburgh and areas south, bringing down trees and power lines with around 200,000 people without power at some point after the storm. Roads were not passable for 2 to 3 days in some locations, and power was not restored to some homes until 3 days after the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong low pressure developed off the Mid Atlantic Coast early on February 25th.  This low deepened considerably and moved slowly up the East Coast on the 25th and 26th.  A trough stretching northwest from this low lingered over the Upper Ohio Valley from February 25th into the 27th.  Cold north to northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie in association with the trough caused lake effect snow showers to develop and intensify on the 25th.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow then affected portions of Northeast Ohio through the afternoon hours of the 26th.  The snow finally diminished during the evening hours of 26th.  Snowfall totals over portions of Northeast Ohio exceeded a foot on the 25th and 26th.  Northwest winds gusted to more than 30 mph on both the 25th and 26th.  Peak gusts of around 35 mph occurred during the early morning hours of the 26th.  The wind and snow combined to create near blizzard conditions at times with visibilities less than one quarter mile.   Drifts up to three feet deep were reported at a few locations.  Some light freezing rain and drizzle also mixed with the snow at times during the afternoon of the 26th.  A slight coating of ice was reported at a few locations.  This storm made travel very difficult and many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow showers developed in Ashtabula County during the morning hours of February 25th.  Periods of moderate and occasionally heavy snow were then reported into the afternoon hours of the 26th.  Areas of light freezing rain and drizzle mixed with the snow showers during the afternoon of the 26th.  A slight glazing of ice was reported at a few locations.  More light snow fell late in the afternoon and during the evening of the 26th.  The snow showers finally tapered to flurries late in the evening.  In addition, northwest winds gusted to more than 30 mph on both the 25th and 26th.  Peak gusts of around 35 mph occurred during the early morning hours of the 26th.  The wind and snow combined to create near blizzard conditions at times with visibilities less than one quarter mile.  Snowfall rates at times exceeded an inch per hour.  Snow totals across inland Ashtabula County during this event ranged from around 6 inches near the southern border to more than 15 inches across the east central portion of the county.  This storm made travel very difficult and many accidents were reported.

A strong low pressure system over the northeastern portion of the country pushed cold air into the Ohio Valley, resulting in a period of snow showers. Gusty northwest winds of 15 to 20 mph with gusts to 30 mph caused some blowing and drifting through the early morning hours of the 27th. One to two inches of snow fell on average, with a few 4 inch or higher amounts being recorded due to localized drifting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county garage in Lancaster measured 4 inches of snow on the evening of the 26th. A spotter located 2.7 northeast of Pickerington measured 3.3 inches the following morning.

Gusty northwest winds of 15 to 20 mph with gusts to 30 mph caused some blowing and drifting through the early morning hours of the 27th. One to two inches of snow fell on average, with a few 4 inch or higher amounts being recorded due to localized drifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure moving north from the Gulf of Mexico on February 5th combined with low pressure across the Ohio Valley and intensified over the Mid-Atlantic states. This produced an historic snow storm across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and eastern Ohio from the afternoon of February 5th to the afternoon of February 6th. Over 2 feet of snow fell across portions of Garrett county Maryland, and in Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia, with locally 3 feet in some locations. Storm totals ranged from 37 inches in Friendsville, MD to 22 inches in Terra Alta, WV. Across Pennsylvania, 18 to 24 inches of snow fell roughly between an area from SR 422 and I-70, with 21.1 inches of snow falling in Pittsburgh. This was the 4th largest snowfall on record in Pittsburgh. Over 2 feet of snow fell in the ridges of Westmoreland and Fayette counties in Pennsylvania. Along and north of I-80 in Pennsylvania 6 to 18 inches of snow fell. South of I-70 in Pennsylvania, eastern Ohio and northern West Virginia amounts ranged from 10 to 20 inches. North of I-70, in eastern Ohio, snow accumulated 12 to 18 inches. The snow was wet and heavy in Pittsburgh and areas south, bringing down trees and power lines with around 200,000 people without power at some point after the storm. Roads were not passable for 2 to 3 days in some locations, and power was not restored to some homes until 3 days after the storm.

An area of strong low pressure moved north from the Gulf Coast to Kentucky on February 5th.  The low lingered over eastern Kentucky from much of the 6th before shifting to the Mid Atlantic Coast late in the day.  Snow associated with this low spread north across northern Ohio during the afternoon of the 5th.  The snow began in Knox and Morrow Counties just before midday and reached the U.S. Route 30 corridor around 2 pm.  By late afternoon the snow had reached the southern suburbs of Cleveland.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow were reported from the later part of the afternoon through around daybreak on the 6th.  The snow quickly ended from northwest to southeast during the morning of the 6th.  Northeast winds of 15 to 25 mph accompanied the snow.  Winds gusted to more than 30 mph at times during the late evening hours of the 5th and early morning hours of the 6th.  Near blizzard conditions with visibilities less than a quarter mile were reported at times.  Snowfall rates exceeded an inch per hour for much of this event.  The heaviest snow fell from the Mansfield area northeast into the Youngstown and Warren areas.  More than a foot of snow fell in that area with a few locations reporting as much as 16 inches of snow.  Accumulations dropped off slightly further north and south.  Considerable blowing and drifting was also reported.  Travel during the peak of this event was nearly impossible and many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow spread into Lorain County from the south during the middle afternoon hours of February 5th and then increased in intensity during the evening hours.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow then continued through the early morning hours of the 6th.  The snow eventually tapered off around daybreak on the 6th.  Snowfall rates at times exceeded an inch per hour.  Northeast winds gusted to as much as 30 mph from the evening of the 5th through the early morning hours of the 6th.  The snow and strong winds combined to create near blizzard conditions with visibilities less than one quarter mile at times.  Snowfall totals in Lorain County during this event ranged from around six inches in the north to nearly a foot along the southern border.  Storm total reports received from snow spotters included: 9.1 inches at Oberlin; 7.5 inches in Carlisle Township; 6.5 inches at North Ridgeville and 6.0 inches at Sheffield Lake.  A cooperative observer south of Elyria measured an event total of 8.0 inches.  Snow drifts over two feet deep were reported in southern Lorain County and travel was nearly impossible at the peak of this event.  Many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed south of the area, pulling in colder air and changing rain to snow overnight.  A half inch to an inch of snow was common across the area, with isolated locations getting a very heavy, wet accumulation of 3 to 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inches of snow was measured in New Carlisle, while four inches was measured in Springfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure centered over Tennessee at daybreak on February 9th moved rapidly northeast during the day.  By early evening, the low was centered over Central Ohio.  The low continued to move northeast during the evening hours and exited the state just after midnight on February 10th.  Snow associated with this low spread into northern Ohio a couple hours before sunrise on the 9th.  The snow continued most of the 9th before briefly tapering to flurries or freezing drizzle during the evening hours.  Light to moderate snow resumed before midnight on the 10th and then continued into the morning hours.  The snow tapered to flurries in the Toledo area late in the morning, but didn't quit in the Mansfield area till late afternoon.  The heaviest snow fell in Northwest Ohio where accumulations in Lucas County topped a foot.  Storm total accumulations dropped off considerably to the south and east of Toledo.  Heavy snow was also reported in Ashtabula County where Lake Erie helped to enhance the snowfall.  Snow began in Ashtabula County during the afternoon of the 9th and then increased in intensity during the early morning hours of the 10th when winds became westerly.  Heavy snow was reported during the morning hours of the 10th with the snow finally tapering off during the evening.  Local accumulations of more than 10 inches were reported in Ashtabula County.   Winds across northern Ohio went from easterly to southwest or west during the evening hours of the 9th as the low moved through.  Gusts to 25 mph began soon after this transition occurred.  Winds remained strong through the early morning hours of the 10th.  Considerable blowing and drifting was reported in Northwest Ohio making difficult driving conditions even worse.  Many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in Seneca County a couple hours before daybreak on February 9th.  The snow picked up in intensity later in the morning with periods of moderate snow then reported into early evening.  The intensity of the snow lessened during the evening hours with light snow finally ending late in the morning of February 10th.  Snowfall rates at the peak of the storm were around an inch per hour with visibilities less than one half mile.   Total snow accumulations across Seneca County ranged from 5 to 7 inches with the higher amounts in the northwest corner of the county.  A cooperative observer in Tiffin measured 6.0 inches of snow.  East to southeast winds accompanied the snow as it fell.  Winds eventually became southwest and increased during the evening hours of the 9th.  Winds gusted to as much as 25 mph from the late evening hours of the 9th through the early morning hours of the 10th.  The winds and snow combined to create considerable blowing and drifting.  Travel was difficult during this storm and several accidents were reported.

Roads were not passable for 2 to 3 days in some locations, and power was not restored to some homes until 3 days after the storm.

An area of strong low pressure moved north from the Gulf Coast to Kentucky on February 5th.  The low lingered over eastern Kentucky from much of the 6th before shifting to the Mid Atlantic Coast late in the day.  Snow associated with this low spread north across northern Ohio during the afternoon of the 5th.  The snow began in Knox and Morrow Counties just before midday and reached the U.S. Route 30 corridor around 2 pm.  By late afternoon the snow had reached the southern suburbs of Cleveland.  Periods of moderate to heavy snow were reported from the later part of the afternoon through around daybreak on the 6th.  The snow quickly ended from northwest to southeast during the morning of the 6th.  Northeast winds of 15 to 25 mph accompanied the snow.  Winds gusted to more than 30 mph at times during the late evening hours of the 5th and early morning hours of the 6th.  Near blizzard conditions with visibilities less than a quarter mile were reported at times.  Snowfall rates exceeded an inch per hour for much of this event.  The heaviest snow fell from the Mansfield area northeast into the Youngstown and Warren areas.  More than a foot of snow fell in that area with a few locations reporting as much as 16 inches of snow.  Accumulations dropped off slightly further north and south.  Considerable blowing and drifting was also reported.  Travel during the peak of this event was nearly impossible and many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow spread into Stark County from the south during the early afternoon hours of February 5th and then increased in intensity during the middle part of the afternoon.  Moderate to heavy snow then continued through daybreak on the 6th with the snow eventually ending during the morning hours.  Snowfall rates during much of this event exceeded an inch per hour.  Northeast winds gusted to as much as 30 mph from the evening of the 5th through the early morning hours of the 6th.  The snow and strong winds combined to create near blizzard conditions with visibilities less than one quarter mile at times.  Snowfall totals during this event ranged from 10 to 14 inches across most of Stark County.  A cooperative observer in Louisville reported a storm total of 13.9 inches.  A snow spotter in Alliance measured 13 inches of snow during this event.  Drifts as much as three feet deep were reported in some locations.  Travel was nearly impossible at the peak of this event.  Many accidents were reported.

An area of low pressure centered over Tennessee at daybreak on February 9th moved rapidly northeast during the day.  By early evening, the low was centered over Central Ohio.  The low continued to move northeast during the evening hours and exited the state just after midnight on February 10th.  Snow associated with this low spread into northern Ohio a couple hours before sunrise on the 9th.  The snow continued most of the 9th before briefly tapering to flurries or freezing drizzle during the evening hours.  Light to moderate snow resumed before midnight on the 10th and then continued into the morning hours.  The snow tapered to flurries in the Toledo area late in the morning, but didn't quit in the Mansfield area till late afternoon.  The heaviest snow fell in Northwest Ohio where accumulations in Lucas County topped a foot.  Storm total accumulations dropped off considerably to the south and east of Toledo.  Heavy snow was also reported in Ashtabula County where Lake Erie helped to enhance the snowfall.  Snow began in Ashtabula County during the afternoon of the 9th and then increased in intensity during the early morning hours of the 10th when winds became westerly.  Heavy snow was reported during the morning hours of the 10th with the snow finally tapering off during the evening.  Local accumulations of more than 10 inches were reported in Ashtabula County.   Winds across northern Ohio went from easterly to southwest or west during the evening hours of the 9th as the low moved through.  Gusts to 25 mph began soon after this transition occurred.  Winds remained strong through the early morning hours of the 10th.  Considerable blowing and drifting was reported in Northwest Ohio making difficult driving conditions even worse.  Many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in Ashland County just before daybreak on February 9th.  The snow picked up in intensity later in the morning with periods of moderate to heavy snow then reported into early evening.  The intensity of the snow lessened during the evening hours with light snow finally ending late in the afternoon on February 10th.  Some patchy freezing drizzle was also reported during the evening of the 9th.  Snowfall rates at the peak of the storm were around an inch per hour with visibilities less than one half mile.  Total snow accumulations across Ashland County ranged from 6 to 8 inches.  East to southeast winds accompanied the snow as it fell and helped to locally enhance the snowfall.  This enhancement resulted from the winds blowing from lower elevations toward higher elevations.  Winds eventually became southwest and increased during the evening hours of the 9th.  Winds gusted to as much as 25 mph from the late evening hours of the 9th through the early morning hours of the 10th.  The winds and snow combined to create considerable blowing and drifting.  Travel was difficult during this storm and several accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across southern Ohio on February 15th.  Snow associated with this low spread into northern Ohio during the middle and later portion of the afternoon. Locations along and east of Interstate 71 and south of U.S. Route 30 saw more than six inches of snow during this event.  More than ten inches of snow was reported in portions of Knox and Holmes Counties.  Visibilities at times were less than one half mile and travel was difficult during this storm.  Several accidents were reported along Interstate 71 at the southern end of Morrow County.  Many accidents were also reported elsewhere in the area.

A very strong area of deepening low pressure moved east across northern Ohio during the evening hours of May 7th.  By midday on the 8th, the low had reached northern New York state.  Showers and thunderstorms associated with the low moved across northern Ohio during the evening hours of the 7th.  Several of the thunderstorms became severe and at least one tornado was reported.  An EF1 tornado moved across portions of eastern Wood County damaging a few homes and buildings.  A downburst caused considerable damage in Erie County.  There were dozens of reports of large hail and damaging winds elsewhere across northern Ohio.  Estimates have indicated that as many as 5,000 homes and businesses in northern Ohio may have sustained damage during this event.  At the peak of the storm more than 300,000 residents were without power in Northeast Ohio alone.  A strong cold front trailing the low exited Ohio to the east early on May 8th.  Gusty southwest to west winds occurred behind this front.  Some wind damage was reported in a few northern Ohio counties during the early afternoon hours of the 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to quarter sized hail was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched downed in rural Wood County near the intersection of Portage and Emerson Roads in Montgomery Township.  The tornado traveled north northeast approximately two and a half miles before lifting near the intersection of Kenner Road and U.S. Route 6 near New Rochester.   Five barns were leveled along the damage path which had a maximum width of around 100 yards.  Several homes along the damage path also sustained roof damage.  Two grain bins were also destroyed.  Many trees and limbs were downed along the damage path.

By midday on the 8th, the low had reached northern New York state.  Showers and thunderstorms associated with the low moved across northern Ohio during the evening hours of the 7th.  Several of the thunderstorms became severe and at least one tornado was reported.  An EF1 tornado moved across portions of eastern Wood County damaging a few homes and buildings.  A downburst caused considerable damage in Erie County.  There were dozens of reports of large hail and damaging winds elsewhere across northern Ohio.  Estimates have indicated that as many as 5,000 homes and businesses in northern Ohio may have sustained damage during this event.  At the peak of the storm more than 300,000 residents were without power in Northeast Ohio alone.  A strong cold front trailing the low exited Ohio to the east early on May 8th.  Gusty southwest to west winds occurred behind this front.  Some wind damage was reported in a few northern Ohio counties during the early afternoon hours of the 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a large tree.  The tree struck a home and caused enough damage for the house to be uninhabitable.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm downburst winds estimated to be at least 60 mph caused damage throughout northern Wood County.  Damage was reported from the Grand Rapids area northeast across Perrysburg Township to near Millbury in the northeast corner of the county.  Several barns and outbuildings were damaged in the Dowling area and a mobile home in Millbury was heavily damaged.  Several homes south of Perrysburg sustained damage from fallen trees.  At least one home in Rossford also sustained damage.  Hundreds of trees and limbs along with a few utility poles were downed across northern Wood County.  Scattered power outages were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm downburst winds produced a swath of damage across northern Ashtabula County.  Damage stretched from the Jefferson area northeastward across Sheffield Township and into the Conneaut area.  Significant tree damage was reported in Jefferson.  A residence in Sheffield Township along Peebles Road had nearly 70 trees toppled.  Dozens of trees were also downed in the Conneaut area with at least five homes in the town heavily damaged by fallen trees.  Scattered downed trees were reported across the remainder of the area.  Many power outages were reported.

Several of the thunderstorms became severe and at least one tornado was reported.  An EF1 tornado moved across portions of eastern Wood County damaging a few homes and buildings.  A downburst caused considerable damage in Erie County.  There were dozens of reports of large hail and damaging winds elsewhere across northern Ohio.  Estimates have indicated that as many as 5,000 homes and businesses in northern Ohio may have sustained damage during this event.  At the peak of the storm more than 300,000 residents were without power in Northeast Ohio alone.  A strong cold front trailing the low exited Ohio to the east early on May 8th.  Gusty southwest to west winds occurred behind this front.  Some wind damage was reported in a few northern Ohio counties during the early afternoon hours of the 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail was observed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm downburst with estimated peak wind gusts of between 70 and 80 mph caused extensive damage in western Erie County.  The downburst damage path began southwest of Sandusky along Homegardner Road where a barn was damaged and many trees toppled.  The damage path continued northeast across State Routes 2 and 101 and through the City of Sandusky.  The damage path ended at the Lake Erie shoreline and was approximately seven miles in length.   The most concentrated damage was along State Route 101 between State Route 2 and the Sandusky city limits.  Many trees and tree limbs were downed in this area along with 13 utility poles.  A few homes and buildings sustained roof damage in this area as well.  Damage in the City of Sandusky was sporadic and consisted mainly of downed trees and limbs.  Widespread power outages were reported in the area with at least 10,000 people loosing power.  Fourteen people were stranded at a restaurant and gas station on the southwest side of Sandusky for seven hours because access was blocked by downed power lines.

A downburst caused considerable damage in Erie County.  There were dozens of reports of large hail and damaging winds elsewhere across northern Ohio.  Estimates have indicated that as many as 5,000 homes and businesses in northern Ohio may have sustained damage during this event.  At the peak of the storm more than 300,000 residents were without power in Northeast Ohio alone.  A strong cold front trailing the low exited Ohio to the east early on May 8th.  Gusty southwest to west winds occurred behind this front.  Some wind damage was reported in a few northern Ohio counties during the early afternoon hours of the 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail was reported.  A few vehicles were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms were scattered across eastern Ohio, the northern West Virginia panhandle, and southwest Pennsylvania ahead of a cold front. Significant damaging winds associated with a large bow echo occured along a path from near Steubenville, Ohio to Latrobe, Pennsylvania. From this bow echo the highest recorded wind gusts at ASOS observation sites were 71 MPH at Wheeling, WV, 66 MPH at Allegheny County Airport in West Mifflin, PA, and 86 MPH at Latrobe, PA. At least 150,000 homes lost power at some point during the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported large trees down from near the intersection of Highways 43 and 22 to the Jefferson County Airport and east across the city of Steubenville. 3 hangars at the airport were significantly damaged with loss of roofs and structural damage. Trees and power lines were down across much the the Steubenville area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across northern Ohio during the afternoon hours of May 31st.  A cold front followed during the evening.  Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with both of these fronts.   Slow moving thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall which caused flash flooding in a few areas.  Rainfall rates at times were between two and three inches per hour.  A few locations received more than four inches of rainfall.  The flooding was the worst in western Geauga County were the damage was extensive.  A few storms also became severe with minor damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms moved across portions of Seneca, Wyandot, Marion and Crawford Counties.  Rainfall rates exceeded two inches per hour at times causing many streams and creeks to leave their banks.  A trained spotter measured a storm total of 3.41 inches of rainfall in Bucyrus.   Many roads and streets had to be closed because of flooding.  The flooding was the worst along the U.S. Route 30 corridor in Wyandot and Crawford Counties.  Between one and two feet of swiftly flowing water was reported on State Route 4 in Bucyrus.  At least one vehicle became stranded in the flood waters.  Many homes sustained damage, mainly from basement flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front lingered across central Ohio on June 4th.  Showers and thunderstorms developed to the north of this front and moved across the area.  A few of the thunderstorms became severe.  Many reports of large hail were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a few large tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved east across the Great Lakes region during the afternoon and evening hours of June 27th.  A warm front stretching southeast from the low moved across northern Ohio during the afternoon and evening hours.  Showers and thunderstorms developed along the front with a few of the stronger storms becoming severe.  Very strong winds were associated with many of the thunderstorms.  A 78 mph wind gust was measured in Findlay. A tornado hit Ashtabula County during the evening hours causing some minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny sized hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across northern Ohio during the afternoon hours of May 31st.  A cold front followed during the evening.  Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with both of these fronts.   Slow moving thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall which caused flash flooding in a few areas.
